topic: Switchback experiment carryover identification by de Bruijn schedule certificate. Pre-anchor: closest published anchors are switchback/time-series experiment papers on carryover and design balance, plus classical crossover-design carryover aliasing; closest banked proposals are eid_policy_commutator_phase_v1 and panel_approx_nbhd_interference_v1, neither of which contains a schedule-history incidence frontier.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize exact identification of every binary L-history switchback policy mean by the full-column-rank condition of the cyclic schedule-history incidence matrix.

source_refs.claim: Derives optimal switchback designs under assumed carryover order and studies misspecification, but frames design as minimax/variance optimization rather than exact identification of all L-history means.
source_refs.author: Bojinov, Simchi-Levi, and Zhao

source_refs.claim: Introduces de Bruijn cycles as maximally compact arbitrary-level counterbalanced stimulus sequences for history/context studies, outside switchback causal identification.
source_refs.author: Aguirre, Mattar, and Magis-Weinberg

source_refs.excerpt: dynamic switch-on ATT functionals in a Markov treatment-history panel have a sharp spectral frontier
every strict gap... has a finite certificate supported on the capacitated incidence system

open_problems.why_unsolved: The switchback literature identifies optimal or robust designs under a chosen carryover order, and de Bruijn cycles give compact counterbalancing in experimental neuroscience, but neither source class states a causal observation map from a cyclic treatment schedule to the full vector of L-history policy means. The nearest banked proposals have Markov-history row-space and incidence-certificate language, yet they explicitly stop short of the schedule-history incidence frontier requested here.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to cite de Bruijn counterbalancing fails because Aguirre-Mattar-Magis-Weinberg use it for stimulus-history balance and BOLD power, while Bojinov-Simchi-Levi-Zhao optimize switchback MSE under carryover-order assumptions rather than proving that observed switchback means span all primitive L-history policy means.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient identification formula defining the cyclic L-history incidence matrix, proving that all L-history policy means are identified exactly when this matrix has full column rank, and displaying the de Bruijn cycle as a full-rank witness.

open_problems.open_problem: Compute finite alias certificates for common alternating switchback schedules that leave observed history means unchanged while changing sustained-regime policy contrasts.

source_refs.claim: Shows existing unbiased switchback estimators require a prespecified influence period, often guessed, and proposes a test for underestimated carryover duration.
source_refs.author: Missault and Masoero
source_refs.venue: NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on Uncovering Causality in Science

source_refs.claim: Studies misspecified carryover order for switchback designs but does not provide deterministic schedule-nullspace witnesses for the unidentified policy contrasts.
source_refs.author: Bojinov, Simchi-Levi, and Zhao

source_refs.excerpt: Construct a finite two-time binary transition-kernel witness for noncommutation

open_problems.why_unsolved: Recent carryover-detection work explains that underestimating the influence period biases switchback estimators and offers a statistical test, while the banked Markov proposal names near-alternation collapse and the commutator proposal asks for finite binary witnesses. The missing object is a deterministic schedule-history nullspace vector, such as the L=2 alternating-schedule delta that changes all-zero versus all-one history means but leaves observed alternating histories fixed.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to compare estimators under larger lag bounds fails because Missault-Masoero note that detection power depends on the effect pattern and distant isolated lags, so the test does not identify the exact nullspace direction that aliases a sustained-regime contrast.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite certificate algorithm that takes a binary schedule and lag L, returns either a full-rank history-incidence table or a nonzero alias vector, and works out the 0011 versus 0101 L=2 table explicitly.

open_problems.open_problem: Separate classical crossover residual balance from full L-history switchback incidence by showing which balance criteria do and do not identify all binary history-regime means.

source_refs.claim: Provides classical treatment-sequence constructions balanced for residual effects of preceding treatments, the main crossover carryover comparator.
source_refs.author: Williams
source_refs.venue: Australian Journal of Scientific Research Series A

source_refs.claim: Compares switch-back and balanced residual designs and studies robustness when treatments have residual as well as main effects.
source_refs.author: Oman and Seiden

source_refs.claim: Pedagogical crossover-design source notes that avoiding aliasing with first-order carryover does not guarantee no aliasing with higher-order carryover effects.
source_refs.author: Penn State STAT 509

source_refs.excerpt: study dynamic causal effects and switchback designs under persistence or carryover
not a non-equivalence between named estimators

open_problems.why_unsolved: Classical crossover designs have rich balance theory for direct and residual effects, including switch-back/residual comparisons, but those criteria equalize lower-order predecessor effects rather than identify an entire L-bit history-mean table for a single aggregate time-series switchback. The prior Markov and interference-bank notes reinforce the comparator risk: without an explicit named-workflow separation, a frontier can collapse into generic panel row-space language.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to import first-order crossover balance fails because the crossover notes explicitly warn that first-order carryover balance does not prevent higher-order carryover aliasing, while the switchback target requires coverage of every primitive L-bit history rather than balanced predecessor labels.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A comparator theorem giving finite examples where classical residual balance holds but the L-history incidence matrix is rank-deficient, alongside conditions under which a de Bruijn-style schedule supplies the missing history coverage.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend the schedule-history incidence frontier to multi-unit or clustered switchback experiments and determine how units can trade off against period length for exact L-history identification.

source_refs.claim: Introduces regular balanced switchback designs that randomize over time and items, with conditions for resilience to carryover, but focuses on balanced multi-unit estimation performance.
source_refs.author: Missault, Masoero, Delbe, Richardson, and Imbens

source_refs.claim: Sequentially rerandomizes switchbacks for balance on prognostic variables and extends to first-order carryover with previous-treatment blocks.
source_refs.author: Zeng, Adjaho, Bucarey, Qin, Zhang, Hoban, Johari, and Wager

source_refs.excerpt: Degree-cap feasibility is equivalently the capacitated incidence system
temporal reversible-spell carryover rather than cross-unit exposure interference

open_problems.why_unsolved: Multi-unit switchback work now studies regular balance, rerandomization, and blocked first-order carryover strata, while banked incidence artifacts cover network exposure feasibility and temporal reversible-spell carryover separately. No collected source states the exact aggregate cluster-time history-incidence condition, or the minimal number of unit-period observations needed to identify all nonparametric L-history means.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to stack units into a larger de Bruijn schedule fails because regular balanced switchback papers balance treatment counts over time and items, while network-incidence certificates are shared across edges or exposure labels and do not preserve the cyclic ordered-history constraints of carryover.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A rank or matching-style characterization of the aggregate unit-period L-history incidence matrix, including a lower bound on total observed unit-period histories and a construction showing when multiple units can shorten each unit schedule below 2^L.

literature_map:
Bojinov, Simchi-Levi, and Zhao (Management Science 2023) is the core switchback anchor: optimal design under assumed carryover order, misspecification analysis, randomization inference, and finite-population CLT.
Missault and Masoero (2025) make the influence-period problem explicit: unbiased switchback estimators require a prespecified upper bound, often guessed, and underestimated carryover causes bias.
Hu and Wager (arXiv 2022, revised 2025) show standard switchbacks can suffer carryover bias under geometric mixing and that burn-in improves rates, which is adjacent but not exact finite-history coverage.
Xiong, Chin, and Taylor (SSRN/arXiv 2024) decompose switchback error into carryover, periodicity, serial correlation, and simultaneous-experiment effects, emphasizing design tradeoffs.
Regular Balanced Switchback Designs (Missault, Masoero, Delbe, Richardson, Imbens 2025) and Sequentially-Rerandomized Switchbacks (Zeng et al. 2026) push multi-unit balance and first-order carryover, not arbitrary L-history incidence.
Classical crossover and switch-back design papers, including Williams 1949 and Oman-Seiden 1988, study residual-effect balance and robustness to carryover; they are the closest collapse-risk comparators.
Higher-order crossover notes and designs warn that first-order carryover balance does not settle second-order aliasing, which supports a full primitive-history framing.
Aguirre, Mattar, and Magis-Weinberg (NeuroImage 2011) already use de Bruijn cycles for arbitrary-level stimulus-history counterbalance and maximal compactness, so novelty cannot be merely using de Bruijn sequences.
The live gap is the switchback causal identification frontier: a finite observation map from cyclic treatment histories to L-history policy means, with explicit rank and alias certificates.
The strongest comparator-facing witness is L=2: 0011 yields the four histories 00, 01, 11, 10 exactly once, while 0101 observes only 01 and 10 and admits an alias vector changing sustained-regime contrasts.
The study-pipeline IV/Manski/interval-regression gaps were reviewed as orchestrator context but are off-topic for this switchback exact-identification substrate.
prior_proposal_map:
eid_policy_commutator_phase_v1 supplies finite-binary-witness language but concerns ordered longitudinal policy operators, not schedule-history incidence.
panel_approx_nbhd_interference_v1 supplies incidence/certificate and comparator-facing caution, but its object is network exposure/orbit inference rather than temporal carryover schedules.
q1_spectral_phase_transition_p1_markov is the closest switchback/carryover banked artifact; it uses Markov history row-space and near-alternation collapse, not de Bruijn coverage.
pid_network_exposure_v1 and pid_network_exposure_aliasing_graphpair_v1 give finite certificate/minimal-witness patterns useful only as incidence analogies.
pid_reversible_dynamic_spillover_v1 is adjacent on temporal carryover and finite-window frontiers, but it is partial-ID bounds rather than exact schedule design.
No inspected prior proposal already states the L=2 0011 identity-incidence table or the alternating 0101 delta witness.
The mined reusable gap is therefore a temporal schedule-history incidence frontier with explicit de Bruijn minimality and comparator-facing alias certificates.
Unrelated IV/Manski/interval study-pipeline gaps were not used for candidate extraction.
